text: Peak velocity achieved during a VO2max test is the strongest predictor of 50-km trail race performance in men, explaining 96% of variance when combined with baseline blood pressure.
Peak velocity achieved during a VO2max test and greater body mass loss during racing are the best predictors of 50-km trail race performance in women, explaining 87% of variance.
For 80-km trail races, peak velocity achieved during a VO2max test is the only significant physiological predictor of performance, explaining 78% of variance.

For 160-km trail races, no classical physiological determinants (aerobic fitness, running economy, body composition, hematocrit) significantly predict performance, suggesting non-physiological factors like pacing strategy, nutrition, and mental resilience dominate.

Greater body mass loss during a 50-km trail race is associated with better performance, suggesting faster runners may tolerate or incur more fluid/mass loss rather than it being detrimental at this distance.
The predictive power of classical physiological parameters (VO2max, running economy, cardiovascular health) diminishes as ultramarathon distance increases from 50 km to 160 km.
specific_value: r2 drops from 0.96 (50 km) to 0.78 (80 km) to non-significant (160 km)

text: Higher training volumes and better cardiovascular health (including lower baseline blood pressure) are associated with better 50-km trail race performance.
